text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CLINIC NOTE
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Department: Medical Oncology / Melanoma Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine oncology follow-up, 24-month mark from diagnosis. Tumor board discussion and shared decision-making regarding treatment discontinuation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Castellano is a 56-year-old female landscape architect with a history of stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant nodular melanoma, originally diagnosed approximately 24 months ago. She presents today for a scheduled visit that was coordinated with multidisciplinary tumor board review in advance of this appointment.

By way of brief background: she initially presented with nodular primary melanoma with metastatic disease to liver and lymph nodes at diagnosis. Staging workup at the time was notable for a CT scan demonstrating hepatic and nodal disease; brain MRI was negative. NGS demonstrated BRAF V600E mutation with elevated TMB at 18.4 mutations/megabase. She was started on dabrafenib plus trametinib (dab/tram) as first-line therapy given the BRAF-mutant status. She had an early partial response at four weeks. This was complicated by febrile pyrexia, which was managed conservatively. Restaging at three months confirmed partial response with ongoing shrinkage; at six months, disease was stable. At approximately eight months, however, she had progression with new brain metastases identified on MRI.

At that point, she was referred to radiation oncology and underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to the brain lesions. She was then switched to ipilimumab plus nivolumab (ipi/nivo, Opdivo/Yervoy). After cycle 1 she was seen in follow-up with concerns for GI toxicity; she was hospitalized for grade 3 immune-mediated colitis, managed with high-dose corticosteroids and ultimately with infliximab. She recovered fully and ipilimumab was discontinued. She continued on Opdivo (nivolumab) maintenance monotherapy thereafter.

At 13 months she was responding on Opdivo. She developed hypothyroidism, consistent with immune-related thyroiditis, and was started on levothyroxine, which has kept her thyroid function stable. Restaging imaging at 15 months and again at 20 months showed progressive deepening of response -- the 20-month scan was notable for deep partial response with marked shrinkage of hepatic and nodal disease and stable brain. At that visit she was ECOG 0 and had returned to full-time work as a landscape architect.

She was seen in clinic today and restaging imaging was reviewed as part of today's tumor board. The most recent CT demonstrates near-complete response -- residual hepatic foci measuring 0.4-0.5 cm, felt to be at the limit of CT resolution and possibly representing scar tissue or treated necrotic disease. Brain MRI remains stable with no active intracranial lesions.

The case was presented at multidisciplinary tumor board attended by medical oncology, neurooncology, radiation oncology, and radiology. The board discussed at length the rationale for treatment pause at this juncture given: (1) cumulative immunotherapy duration now reaching approximately two years total from initiation of ipi/nivo; (2) near-complete systemic response with residual lesions of uncertain viability; (3) brain metastases stable and well-controlled following SRS with no new lesions on serial imaging; (4) patient's clear expressed preference to explore stopping therapy. The consensus recommendation from the board was to complete the current nivolumab cycle and then hold therapy, with transition to active observation and surveillance imaging every three months.

This recommendation and its rationale were discussed in detail with Ms. Castellano during today's visit. She was counseled on the rationale for pausing Opdivo, the available data supporting treatment holidays after deep response in patients on checkpoint inhibitor therapy, and the fact that retreatment remains a viable option should she relapse. She was also counseled at length about signs and symptoms of recurrence to monitor for, including new skin lesions, unexplained lymphadenopathy, neurological changes, abdominal discomfort, and unexplained weight loss or fatigue.

She was emotionally relieved by this plan. She expressed that the biweekly infusion schedule has been burdensome and that she is eager to resume a more normal life. She asked about long-term prognosis and was counseled that while data are encouraging, we cannot guarantee durable remission off therapy, and vigilant follow-up remains essential. She understood and verbalized this clearly.

Currently, she reports no new symptoms. No headaches, visual changes, or neurological complaints. No new skin lesions she has noticed. GI function normal. Fatigue minimal, improved significantly compared to earlier in treatment. No joint pain or rash. Energy level good; she is back to full-time landscaping work and has been able to resume outdoor activities.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: mild fatigue (baseline, improved), mild cold intolerance (on levothyroxine, stable).
Negative: no headache, no focal neurological symptoms, no diplopia, no nausea/vomiting, no diarrhea or change in bowel habits, no abdominal pain, no new skin changes noted by patient, no lymphadenopathy perceived, no shortness of breath, no chest pain, no hematuria, no dysuria, no unintentional weight loss.

---

PAST MEDICAL / ONCOLOGIC HISTORY
1. Stage IV melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, nodular subtype -- as above
2. Immune-related colitis (grade 3, on ipi/nivo, resolved after corticosteroids and infliximab)
3. Immune-related hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine, stable)
4. History of febrile pyrexia on dab/tram (managed conservatively)
5. Brain metastases s/p SRS (stable, no further radiation)

---

MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily (immune-related hypothyroidism)
2. Nivolumab (Opdivo) -- FINAL CYCLE per today's plan
3. Vitamin D supplementation

---

ALLERGIES
NKDA. Prior immune-related events as above (colitis, hypothyroidism) -- not listed as allergies per se, context-dependent.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY
Married, lives with spouse. Landscape architect, practicing full-time. Non-smoker. Social alcohol use only. No illicit substances. Resides in southwestern US; significant occupational sun exposure history. Strong family support system; husband attends most visits.

---

FAMILY HISTORY
Mother with breast cancer (age 68, survived). No known family history of melanoma or other skin cancers. No known BRCA or other germline cancer predisposition syndromes in family.

---

PHYSICAL EXAM
Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 64, Temp 98.4F, SpO2 98% RA, Weight stable.
General: Well-appearing, healthy affect, no acute distress. Appears well. In good spirits today.
Skin: No new pigmented lesions appreciated on cursory exam. No rash, no erythema. Prior biopsy site scar well-healed.
HEENT: Normocephalic. Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx clear. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpated.
Cardiovascular: RRR, no murmurs.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. No palpable abdominal masses.
Extremities: No edema. No calf tenderness.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. CN II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Normal gait.

---

LAB RESULTS
Most recent labs reviewed (drawn prior to today's visit):
- CBC: WBC 6.2, Hgb 12.9, Plt 198 -- all within normal limits
- BMP: Creatinine 0.82, electrolytes normal, LFTs unremarkable (AST/ALT within normal limits)
- TSH: 2.1 (within normal range on current levothyroxine dose -- stable, no dose adjustment needed)
- LDH: within normal limits

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (most recent, reviewed at tumor board today): Near-complete response. Residual hepatic foci 0.4-0.5 cm, at limit of CT resolution, appearance consistent with scar/treated disease versus minimal residual viable tumor. Previously bulky hepatic and nodal disease has essentially resolved. No new sites of disease. No pleural effusions. No new pulmonary nodules.

MRI brain (most recent, reviewed at tumor board today): Stable. No new intracranial lesions. Previously treated brain met sites show post-SRS changes without evidence of progression. No leptomeningeal enhancement.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Castellano is a 56-year-old woman with stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant nodular melanoma now approximately 24 months from diagnosis. She has achieved a near-complete response on nivolumab (Opdivo) maintenance following an impressive treatment course: initial response to dab/tram, progression at 8 months with new brain mets treated with SRS, conversion to ipi/nivo complicated by grade 3 colitis requiring infliximab, then Opdivo maintenance with deep ongoing response. Brain remains stable post-SRS. Residual hepatic lesions at 0.4-0.5 cm are of uncertain viability -- radiology feels these are most consistent with scar tissue given their stability and diminutive size at this juncture.

1. TREATMENT DISCONTINUATION
Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion today, consensus is to complete the current Opdivo cycle and hold further nivolumab therapy. Key factors supporting this decision:
   - Approximately 24 months of cumulative immunotherapy from initiation of induction ipi/nivo; current data suggest two years is a reasonable and well-supported duration
   - Near-complete response with residual disease at limit of imaging resolution
   - Brain metastases stable following SRS, no active intracranial disease
   - Good performance status (ECOG 0), minimal toxicity burden currently
   - Patient preference to discontinue -- clearly articulated, well-informed request
   Retreatment with Opdivo remains an option and would be considered promptly at any sign of relapse. Patient counseled that ~50% of patients who have deep response and stop checkpoint therapy remain progression-free long term, though variability exists; her BRAF status also provides an additional line of therapy (targeted rechallenge with BRAF/MEK inhibitors) if needed.

2. SURVEILLANCE PLAN
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 3 months x 1 year off therapy, then reassess frequency
   - MRI brain every 3 months x 1 year (given prior brain met history, continued close CNS surveillance warranted)
   - TSH every 6 months while on levothyroxine; continue current dose
   - Dermatology referral to be coordinated for routine full-body skin surveillance
   - Patient instructed to call with any new symptoms without waiting for scheduled visit: new headache, vision changes, neurological symptoms, new skin lesions, palpable lumps, abdominal discomfort, significant weight loss, or return of fatigue

3. HYPOTHYROIDISM
   TSH stable at 2.1 on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No dose changes. Continue current regimen. Recheck TSH in 6 months or sooner if symptomatic.

4. PSYCHOSOCIAL
   Patient was visibly relieved and emotional during this conversation in a positive sense. She mentioned this has been a difficult two years and she is looking forward to "getting her life back." She was reassured that our team remains closely engaged and that the surveillance plan is designed to catch any recurrence early. Social work support offered; she declined at this time but was given contact information. Husband present and engaged.

5. FOLLOW-UP
   Next visit in approximately 3 months, at which time first off-therapy surveillance imaging will be reviewed. Sooner as needed.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care today: approximately 50 minutes.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Oncologist, MD]
Medical Oncology / Melanoma Program
